image_sources.description: Iconic exterior view of Kubuswoningen showing the row of tilted yellow cubes on hexagonal pylons
image_sources.useful_for: Main exterior reference - yellow cladding, cube tilt angle, overall massing and rhythm of repeated units

image_sources.description: Street-level exterior view showing the full complex with pedestrian walkway underneath
image_sources.useful_for: Understanding the elevated walkway/bridge structure and how cubes sit above street level - key for Minecraft foundation layout

image_sources.description: View from below showing hexagonal concrete pylons supporting the tilted cubes
image_sources.useful_for: Hexagonal pylon structure reference - critical for building the tree-trunk supports in Minecraft

image_sources.description: Close-up of yellow/grey cladding panels and window placement on cube facade
image_sources.useful_for: Window pattern and cladding detail - helps determine block palette and window placement in Minecraft

image_sources.description: Interior view of the angled living room with tilted walls and windows on the ground floor of a cube
image_sources.useful_for: Interior angled room reference - shows how tilted walls create the diamond-shaped living space

image_sources.description: Interior photos from the Kijk-Kubus show cube museum unit, all three levels visible
image_sources.useful_for: Official museum cube interior - shows furniture placement and how residents live in angled rooms

image_sources.description: Exterior view showing yellow cube houses from Oude Haven waterside

image_sources.useful_for: Comprehensive photo gallery with 20+ images covering all angles - best single source for reference images

image_sources.useful_for: Case study with annotated photos explaining the structural concept

architectural_drawings.credit: Courtesy of Piet Blom Museum via ArchDaily

architectural_drawings.description: Original sketch by Piet Blom showing the concept of tilted cubes as an urban forest / tree canopy
architectural_drawings.credit: Piet Blom / Piet Blom Museum via ArchDaily

architectural_drawings.description: Cross-section drawing showing the three interior levels within the tilted cube and the hexagonal pylon below
Elevation drawing showing the exterior profile of the cube houses
Site plan showing the arrangement of all 38+2 cubes along Overblaak street
Plans, sections and architectural analysis with dimensional annotations
Architectural drawings and detailed analysis of the Kubuswoningen structure
Visual lexicon entry with annotated drawings explaining the cube geometry and tilt

minecraft_building_notes.tilt_approach: The 54.7-degree tilt creates a cube balanced on one vertex. In Minecraft, approximate with a 45-degree rotated cube using staircase blocks and slabs for the angled surfaces. A 7-block-wide cube tilted diagonally is a good scale.
minecraft_building_notes.color_palette: Yellow concrete/terracotta for cladding, gray concrete for pylons, dark gray for window frames, light gray for structural concrete
minecraft_building_notes.pylon_shape: Hexagonal pylons can be approximated with a 3-wide column using stairs to create the hex profile
minecraft_building_notes.interior_levels: Level 1: Living room + kitchen (diamond-shaped floor), Level 2: Two bedrooms + bathroom, Level 3: Pyramid top / small garden
minecraft_building_notes.scale_suggestion: Each cube at roughly 15x15x15 blocks gives enough interior detail. The hex pylon beneath adds 8-10 blocks of height.
key_features_to_capture: Row of repeated tilted cubes along a linear walkway
Yellow cladding with triangular window panels
Elevated pedestrian bridge/walkway connecting all units
Diamond-shaped floor plans visible through windows
